speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's April 9, 1995. And this is tape four of Max Durst's interview. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now in Plaszow. It is probably March or about March-- maybe late February, March-- of 1943. As I mentioned before, I'm working in a grand tailoring shop, otherwise known in German as [GERMAN]. I was in this-- working in this detail for, [SIGHS] I would say, maybe four, maybe even six weeks. And an order came into the camp administration that they need some people to be sent out, outside the camp. They have to build some kind of a dam-- divert water. Water was running the wrong way or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they get-- who do they usually get? They usually get-- the camp administration takes the people that came in last and sends them out. The old-- you always have preference. You have seniority, right? Believe it or not, in an-- in one of these camps, you always had seniority, right, if you're one of the early inmates. If you came in, it did not matter that you were imprisoned or that was imprisoned since 1942. It mattered that I was only in this particular camp for the last six weeks or thereabouts. I was the late comer. So I was the first one-- anytime they needed somebody to be shipped out, I was the first one to go. When you would get shipped out, would you think this is good or this is bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: In most cases, you were not particularly anxious to be shipped out. Here, wherever-- as bad as it was, you knew what it was. Being shipped out created certain uncertainties. Also, here you already had your partnership, the one I talked about before. Now I'm going to be shipped out, I have to look for a new partner. I have to see how I'm going to secure my-- my extracurricular food that we usually had to survive with, in one way or another. So it was a change. And we hated changes, because we really didn't-- now I did remember, famous [NON-ENGLISH] and target practice. Don't let us forget that. And I was not particularly anxious to be shipped out from anywhere. But nonetheless, I was shipped out. And I was shipped out to a very, very famous Polish resort town, known as Zakopane. Now Zakopane is world famous for their skiing. This is high in the mountains. And it-- I believe it's in the-- in the Polish-- the Tatra Mountains. It's a very famous resort. It is even very famous now. There is a lot of tourists going to the place called Zakopane. In any event, we arrived in Zakopane. We were put up in a sort of-- in an abandoned building there. And we were taken out every morning to the mountains to build some kind of a dam. It was actually-- that place was hell on Earth. It was hell on Earth because we were continuously guarded, not so much by our own Jewish sort of foremen, but we were actually guarded by the SS. And there was two or three young SS men-- very, very vicious. And they used to beat the living hell out of us-- very, very brutal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, fortunately, did not get beaten up that often. I may have beaten-- beaten up once or twice, not all that severely. But some of my friends I was with-- they were really beaten up severely. Any time these young SS had a bad night, they took it out on us, for no reason whatsoever.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any event, we were there for several months. And that dam, by the way, never, ever worked. They tried to divert the water. They finally gave up. So what they finally did-- they says, to hell with it. And they took us and they shipped us right back to Plaszow. Now I'm back in Plaszow. We are already in the summer of 1943. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Plaszow is being, for some reason, slowly liquidated. So no-- I am in Plaszow no more than maybe three or four weeks after we return from that labor camp in Zakopane, and they load us up on trains, and we're being shipped either into Germany or Austria. It was not known to us at the time. They loaded us up on trains, and we're being shipped to-- we are on the train. They give us a loaf of bread, a pail of water for the entire-- that one cattle car, which held, I would say, at least 100 people-- completely enclosed, with little vents here and there, enough for the cattle to be able to breath. But this cattle car was living hell. It took us-- we were in that cattle car for seven days-- five-- between five or seven days. I don't remember exactly. But it sound-- it sounded more-- it felt like it was more like seven weeks than the seven days that we traveled. Make frequent stops, and we were just standing there. And the sun was baking on us without mercy.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- and water-- I think that during those seven days, we had the original pail of water. And I think we might have gotten another pail of water somewhere on the way. We never got any more. We got some more bread, but the bread, we never even ate. We were so dehydrated that we just didn't care. We just wanted water. We didn't even care for the bread. It was laying. Can you imagine? We were hungry, but we didn't eat the bread because we were in such-- such dehydrated state that it was absolutely brutal. We finally arrived. And when we arrived what we are-- Can I ask you question?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why did they give you that one loaf of bread for 100 people? What they did they want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, each--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Each got a load of bread. It wasn't one loaf of bread for 100 people. Each of us got a loaf of bread, yes. But we were 100 people in a cattle car, now, and one pail of water. And then when we came to the station, what they did is we needed more water. So what they did is the Germans used to take a hose. And they didn't want to open the cattle car. So they sprayed with the hose into the cattle car, water, which was a blessing by itself, because we all got a little bit water on ourselves. But that water had to be used-- had to be used. Also, we had to catch the water to fill up the water, so we can use it for drinking water. Now there was also another pail, which was used for our personal needs. So-- and that pail was, of course, supposedly emptied every day, or every night, or whatever. But you can well imagine the stench and the conditions from that-- from those people. Now, we were all men. There were no women with us. We were all men. But I remember one particular incident. There was a father. And he was with his young son. He had a young child with him. This was a-- this was from Plaszow, remember. And I mentioned before that for some reason, there were some children in Plaszow. I don't know how they were able to get in there, but they were there, some children. Now this boy must've been, I would say maybe seven, eight years old. And his father desperately tried to make his little boy as comfortable as he could. And I could feel-- I felt for that child, and the father, for that matter. Because you see, I was sort of imagining myself, being so small, and my father trying to protect me from a horde of people. We were 100 people. And then everybody, really-- if you're tightly together, everybody looks out for himself. There is very low consideration for your fellow. Consideration is great. But if you're packed in, 100 people in a cattle car, there really is no consideration. You-- you just look out for yourself and try to be-- try to be as-- as-- try to disturb your next door neighbor as little as possible. But you look out for yourself and try to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were all trying to sort of-- and this father, he was a husky man. And he-- he desperately tried to help this-- this little boy of his. Now I don't know what has happened to them. But I know that when we arrived in Mauthausen, that child never, ever survived. Because in Mauthausen, there were no children. So anyway, we arrived in Mauthausen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We are at Mauthausen maybe no more than two days. And all of a sudden, we get an order to be loaded up into cars all over again. We are loaded up into cars. Now this is already-- we are probably in August of 1943. And we are getting shipped. And what do we know? We don't know where we're being shipped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this time, as bad as it was coming from Plaszow to Mauthausen, it was worse leaving Mauthausen, because we were in the mountains there. And it was even hotter. And again, it took us seven days. And we were shipped to Auschwitz. And we arrive in Auschwitz seven days later. Now, at the time, I think that from that train, there were several dead people in that train that never made it anymore, because we-- we-- it was-- it was absolutely horrendous. That ride back from Mauthausen to Auschwitz was even worse-- if it is imaginable, it was even worse than the ride from Plaszow to Mauthausen, which was bad enough. But you see, the reason it was worse-- it was primarily because you start off in Poland. And Poland is not as hot as if you get further into Austria. And if you get into the mountain region, where the sun starts hitting you harder and harder, plus the fact that it could've been also that the weather has gotten hotter yet. Even-- even the weather condition changed to the fact that it became-- the temperature must have gone up quite a few degrees.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were, at-- again, no water. We got our bread. We got our loaf of bread. That loaf of bread was absolutely worthless. Because what-- all we wanted is water, water, water, and water. It is like being stranded on a desert without any water. Now we finally arrive in Auschwitz. And we knew we were in Auschwitz because we saw the signs, Auschwitz. And we got off. Can I ask you a question?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think allowed you to survive those two seven day trips--

speaker: interviewee
text: What made me survive?

speaker: interviewer
text: --back and forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily youth. I was young. And I tried not to think. I tried to imagine-- always, I tried to tell myself, somehow you'll survive. Somehow you'll survive. That's-- I suppose that's what kept me going. Because you could really get insane from it. It was insa-- it was just-- the suffering was such that, had I been older-- and most of the older people did not survive. But the young ones, for some reason, they-- you have a certain amount of energy at 18 or at 17-- at that time, I was probably about 17-- whereby I was able to survive. Now-- with very little water, but somehow we survived. Now when we arrived in Auschwitz, I was together with a-- with a friend at that time, who was my partner in another camp. And we were in the same train. And as we arrived, we are standing in line, where they segregate you. They have an inspection. People going to the right. People going to the left. I was in the back line. And my friend was next to me. And as luck would have it, a train-- a luxury train arrives on the next track at the station. And these were people which were well dressed, Jews from-- I think it was from Holland. They came to Auschwitz in some kind of a luxury train. They were dressed. They had packages. They had valises. They had luggage. And somehow, they were eating fruit-- apples. And they somehow threw out some-- a part of the apple that they didn't finish, for some reason. They were told later, I found out, that they are going somewhere to some kind of a recreation camp, or whatever. And they threw out. And my friend found a piece of apple. And I found a piece of orange. And we quickly devoured that. He devoured that little piece of apple that somebody threw out from the train, from those people that were brought in from Holland. And I don't believe that they had any intentions of giving it to us. It's just that they finished whatever they finished and the rest they through out, because they were regular windows. They were not in a cattle car. They were in a sort of a passenger train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about them, seeing them that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we felt sorry for them. Because we were already hardened. We knew-- we could survive much more so than they, because we were gradually-- we were gradual. Now, we were-- we started off with labor camps, and then we were getting less, and less, and less, and less. Now here, people come from Holland, which are used to live in a-- in the normal conditions. And they go from sort of from heaven to hell, in one day. We went from-- from heaven to hell in three years. And it was very gradual. Now you get hardened. With each camp, you get a little bit more hardened. And you get slowly accustomed to the conditions. Therefore, for us it was much easier to survive than for somebody who was living a normal life, and here he comes to Auschwitz. I mean, the chances of survival are zero, under these conditions. It's like you jog all your life, and you can jog 10 miles without any big problems. But if you never jogged in your life, you try it, and you're going to drop after the first one or two miles. You will be-- you will never be able to make it. That is a very, very-- this is about as close a comparison as I can give you under the circumstances.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I grabbed my piece of apple or orange. I don't know what it was. It was either one or the other. And my friend-- and we were both standing in line. And I made sure one thing, that before I come to that German that was doing the selecting to the right and to the left-- now what I observed is that the elderly, the one that were walking with their stooped shoulders and their head down, they were automatically put to the left. They were told to go to the left. I also noticed some people who could hardly walk. They had injuries of some kind or another-- to the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I also noticed that the people who looked young and strong, they were told to go to the right. Now I told my friend, I says, look, when we get to that officer, we better look young and strong. Because believe me, I don't think these other people will ever make it. So he straightened out. And he looked him straight in the eye. And to the right. I am next. I'm straight, look him in the eye. He looks at me. To the right. Now as soon as we got to the right, there was the welcoming committee there. The welcome committee was the people, the Jews that worked in Auschwitz. These were the ones that-- that worked the gas chambers, and so on, and so forth. And they were Jews. They were forced to do it. So they-- one of the young fellows that was there, he says, you are the lucky ones. He says, you are-- you are the ones that are going to be alive. The other ones are going into the ovens. They will never survive. Now they have taken us. At the time, I knew that the shower that they were taking us were real showers, because they told us, you are the ones that are the-- you're going to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard about the ovens before that movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes. We knew already. At that time, we were well aware of the crematoria. We saw the crematoria. We saw them. We saw the chimneys. We didn't see the inside of it, but we saw the chimneys. We saw the smoke coming out from the chimneys. So at that time we were-- this was, don't forget, this is-- now we are already in, I think it's August of '43. Or was it 40-- no, I'm sorry. We are now-- I think we are already-- ah, let me see. I think we are already in '44. Are we in '44?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm trying--

speaker: interviewee
text: I may have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we must be already in '44, because we have-- may I backtrack?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We had Rzeszow in '43. Pustkow-- from Pustkow to Plaszow was already '44. I may have said before '43, but I really meant '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we are already in '44. Now, this was 1944. We already knew about crematoria. We knew about extermination. At that time, it was already well known. So we are now in-- in-- I am now in Auschwitz, being selected. They take us to the showers. They were real showers. But bef-- before we go into the showers, we were supplied drinking water. Now the drinking water were-- imagine these large pails, regular pails that you wash floors, which is fine. And they have given us water to drink. And they have several pails there. And it was done by the Jewish young men that were-- that were part of that. I think they were called, at the time, [? Canada. ?] The detail was called [? Canada. ?] [? Canada ?] were the ones that were working the crematoriums. But they were also working-- a certain-- a certain detail of the [? Canada ?] was taking care of us, like taking us the showers, telling us where to put the clothing, giving us the water. So they-- now, I must have finished, without exaggeration, a half a pail of that water. And now I'm talking, I must've consumed a minimum of maybe two gallons of water, at least, if not more. Because I was so dehydrated. And the young men that were there, they told us, drink all the water you can. You will not be short of water. But they says drink, drink, drink. They sort of encouraged us to drink all you can, because water will not hurt you. But drink the water. Now so we drank the water. Then we went into the shower. As soon as we went out of the shower, we got tattooed. And here is my tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Turn your arm please. Good. And read the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: B as in boy, 7771. The B was the later. At first, when they-- now don't forget that this was already probably late August or early September of 1944. The first numbers were without an A, just a number. Then came the A. And then came the B. So we were actually-- my group was already the latecomers to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking at that time, the moment of your tattoo? At 12, you thought you would go to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- honestly, I couldn't care less that I'm being-- now I was being tattooed by one of the members of that [? Canada ?] group. And they were very efficient. They-- I mean, they must have done thousands and thousands and thousands of tattoos. So they were very-- it wasn't even painful or anything. It was just-- now you can imagine the same needle was being used over and over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was sort of fortunate that they didn't have any diseases. Can you imagine what would've happened if this would have been today? [LAUGHS] I mean, I-- I dread to just even think of what would happened if this would have been in this day and age. I guess in those days, there were no-- no diseases, which no-- which could have hurt us. There were other things that hurt us, but diseases was not one of them-- not in this particular case.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any event, we were tattooed. And we were assigned to barracks in Birkenau. Now in Birkenau, I never got to do any work. We were just sitting around and doing nothing. I was in Birkenau no more than maybe three weeks or maybe even four weeks. And again, we got an order to be shipped out somewhere. Here I go again. I'm being shipped out. We're put on trucks. And we were shipped out to a coal mining town called [? Rudultow, ?] which was right on the German border. It was really part of Poland, I believe, but it was on the German border. And there was a coal mine in there. And they needed people to mine coal. I was among the group that was shipped to that camp because, again, I was a late comer. Last one in, first one out. I got to [? Rudultow. ?] And there were already people there. There were already inmates there, and we were the newcomers. And they each-- everybody got assigned to a certain work detail. My assignment, luckily, was not to be in the coal mine. I never saw a coal mine in my life. I was assigned to do work in a delousing detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now let me clarify what delousing detail is. We had our clothing. And our clothing had to be regularly put into a sort of a large kettle, which was-- which was on the outside. And it was under high temperature. It was supposed to kill all the lice that we accumulated, because we couldn't take normal showers. We never took baths. It was just-- we had to-- every week, once a week, all our clothing had to go into that de-- I don't know, what would-- what would you call it? Delousing is right.

speaker: interviewee
text: To make it antiseptic.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was sanitizing, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sanitizing. It was sort of a-- it looked somewhat like a dry cleaning machine that I have seen here in dry cleaning establishments. However, it wasn't a dry cleaning, obviously. It was for the purpose of getting rid of your lice. And lice was a problem. That was a major problem. We were very uncomfortable with-- with-- that was really-- this was like a plague. In any event--

speaker: interviewer
text: What other things were small problems that people may not know about, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Getting stomach problems. You sometimes get a hold of some food that you weren't supposed to eat. And you ate it, because you were hungry. And you got a bad stomach. And you got diarrhea. And if you couldn't cure that diarrhea, that diarrhea could kill you. So this was another problem. And then a problem was also not enough rations, not enough food. And even though they had sort of a so-called hospital, or whatever they want to call it-- they used to call it [NON-ENGLISH], which was a [NON-ENGLISH] for sort of a so-called hospital, which was operated-- but not by doctors. It was operated by medical students, at best-- Jewish medical students that never-- that never-- that had never had a chance to become doctors. But they had a basic knowledge of what to do. And we had a lot of home remedies. Believe it or not, we learned-- I learned that if I have an infection, the way to cure an infection-- we didn't-- in those days, the big item, as I later found out, was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a sort of a salve, called [NON-ENGLISH]. Now [NON-ENGLISH] is you have an infection and you want it to come to head. You put that [NON-ENGLISH] on. And that brought it to it. Now, we didn't have any of that. But we had home remedies. We used to take a piece of potato, raw potato, slice it, put it on your wound. And it did miracles for you. It did the same thing that that [NON-ENGLISH] did. A slice of potato cured-- cured certain things. So we learned to-- how to do home remedies. And they worked. To a great extent, they worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in any event, I was-- my detail was-- I was in charge-- not only in charge, but I was doing the actual work. I was only one doing the work. I had to build up the pressure in that-- in that large kettle, outdoor kettle. And I had to make sure that the pressure was there. I had to load up the clothing, do the cleaning, take it out, put it in a designated spot, put in more cleaning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my work was at night. Now my lucky break was that this kettle, that-- that sanitizing kettle, was located near the kitchen. And at night, the Germans, didn't-- they didn't watch us that closely. So I used to sneak in the kitchen. I befriended the cook, who was also a Jewish man. And while the pressure was building in there, I always had like a half an hour. And in the kitchen-- I mean, the kitchen there was always food. So he used to come in. And he says-- he used to give me all kinds of food. And I used to eat some. And I used to always bring some home, stuff my pockets, bring it-- bring it home. My partners had to be taken care of.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as long as I worked in that-- that sanitizing thing, I was really in heaven there, because I had the best of two worlds. It was easy-- easy work. And the kitchen was nearby. And at night, when nobody saw us, and we could go into the kitchen, and there was always food to be gotten in the kitchen-- was it potatoes, or bread, or whatever. There was never a shortage of food in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever was the cook, he was the-- he was the king of the-- on the road at that time. Because after all, he is the cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't remember his name. But he was a Hungarian Jewish young man-- extremely nice person, very kind, very fair. Never wanted anything in return. [LAUGHS] He just-- whenever he saw he could give somebody food without being caught, he always did it. So the-- he was one of the very, very nice people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
